summary: Jeff Atwood opts out of his usual elaborate geek gift guide due to the 2008 economic crisis, instead offering just two modest recommendations. He enthusiastically endorses the Planet Earth BBC series as the definitive high-definition viewing experience, praising its five-year production and stunning wildlife footage. His second recommendation is the Absolutely Mad DVD collection containing 50 years of Mad magazine issues, which he notes is refreshingly DRM-free. The post reflects a shift from consumerist enthusiasm to more restrained recommendations during uncertain economic times.
key_points: The 2008 economic crisis dampened enthusiasm for typical consumer gift guides
Planet Earth BBC series represents the pinnacle of high-definition video content, justifying the HD format
The Absolutely Mad DVD exemplifies good digital distribution: simple PDFs without DRM or custom software
Donald Knuth contributed to Mad magazine early in his career, writing about the Potrzebie measurement system
Quality content and DRM-free distribution matter more than quantity of gifts
money_quotes: I don't know if it's the global economic apocalypse, or what, but I'm having a hard time mustering the required level of enthusiasm for buying more stuff.
Filmed over five years, at a budget of 25 million dollars, and completely in high definition, the series is nothing less than revelatory.

I'm in awe of the footage they captured, imagining how many months they must have spent to filming get a single moment of an Amur Leopard stalking across the Siberian taiga, one of the last 30 in the world.
As far as I'm concerned, Planet Earth is the definitive high definition experience to date.
I should also mention that the Absolutely Mad DVD is delightfully free of DRM or crazy custom viewing software. Each issue is a simple, unencumbered PDF file in a folder on the disc.
Who knew it took this much work to be stupid?
And if not, well, at least you can use these two shiny discs to blind your enemies.
key_insight: During times of economic uncertainty, the best gifts are those offering lasting value and respectful distribution rather than flashy consumption.
